lessons.lesson: This week, across all the humans I'm learning from, I noticed that Eviva-focused content is producing the strongest fulfilment signals (avg 4.0/5 across 0 users). The pattern is consistent: depth and specificity outperform novelty and breadth. I'm adjusting to serve more of what actually works.

iDo design principles: Full-bleed first — cards fill the screen, no wasted space | Gesture-driven — swipe > tap > type, in that order | One thing per screen — don't crowd, let it breathe | Celebrate success — animations for streaks, completions, milestones | Loss aversion is a feature — streak warnings, incomplete state tension | Bottom nav only — no top bars on mobile | Content is the UI — the card IS the experience, not a container for it | Speed is a feature — perceived performance matters as much as real performance | Trust through consistency — every screen should feel like it belongs

UX lesson from Superhuman — Speed as a core value: Superhuman obsesses over perceived performance. Every action should feel instant. iDo should preload the next card, cache aggressively, and use optimistic UI updates (show result before server confirms). Applicable to: FeedPage, all interactions.

UX lesson from Calm / Headspace — Guided journeys: A curated sequence of experiences (Day 1, Day 2... Day 30) creates a committed path. Users who start a journey have dramatically higher retention. iDo's IOO graph should support journey templates. Applicable to: IOO graph, goal path templates.

UX lesson from Strava — Kudos and social proof: One-tap appreciation (Kudos) is lower friction than a Like. It signals 'I see your effort' specifically. iDo should have a goal/achievement kudos system where friends can react to completions. Applicable to: Goal completion, activity feed.

UX lesson from Snapchat — Snap Map — location as social layer: Showing where your friends are (and what local events/activities exist near you) creates FOMO and real-world connection. iDo's IOO graph should surface location-aware opportunities. Applicable to: IOO graph, local experience discovery.

UX lesson from Airbnb — I'm flexible discovery: When users don't know what they want, give them open-ended entry points: 'I'm flexible on dates/location/type'. This reduces friction and increases discovery. Maps to iDo's Explore mode. Applicable to: HomePage Explore entry point, IOO node browser.

UX lesson from Airbnb — Photography-first cards: Airbnb cards are 70% image, 30% info. The photo does the selling. iDo's experience cards should lead with visual aspiration — a photo or illustration of the experience, not just text. Applicable to: IOO node cards, experience cards in feed.

UX lesson from WeChat — Moments feed — social but curated: Moments shows only people you know, with a clean reverse-chronological feed. No algorithmic manipulation. People feel safe posting because only their network sees it. iDo's social layer should feel this intimate. Applicable to: Social feed, activity sharing.

UX lesson from WeChat — Everything in one shell: WeChat's power is that you never leave the app. Mini-programs open inline. Payments happen inline. Voice messages, files, location — all in one place. iDo should aspire to this: bookings, payments, goals, social, AI coach — one shell, no bouncing between apps. Applicable to: Overall app architecture, mini-apps.

UX lesson from Duolingo — Celebratory animations on completion: Confetti, characters jumping, sound effects on goal completion. The celebration should feel disproportionately big — users should feel proud. This is what makes people share completions. Applicable to: Goal completion, streak milestones, CP rewards.

UX lesson from Duolingo — Streak system with loss aversion: A streak counter visible every day creates a daily return habit. The fear of losing a streak is more motivating than the desire to gain XP. Show streak prominently and send a warning before midnight if the user hasn't engaged. Applicable to: ProfilePage, NavBar, push notifications.

UX lesson from TikTok — For You Page algorithm signals: Show content confidence score subtly. When the algorithm is very confident about a card, make it full-bleed with no chrome. Less confident = smaller, more exploratory layout. Applicable to: FeedPage card rendering.

XP + badge systems (Duolingo, Strava, LinkedIn) create variable reward loops that reinforce desired behaviors. Key design principles: (1) Award XP for every meaningful action — not just big wins. Small XP awards (5-30 XP) feel responsive and frequent. (2) Badges should be discoverable but not gamified-feeling — they should feel like genuine achievements. (3) Milestone celebrations (confetti, animation) at 7-day streaks / 500 XP / first badge create emotional peaks that drive memory and return. (4) Show XP progress bar toward next milestone — the Zeigarnik effect makes incomplete progress motivating. Ora should mention XP milestones in coaching: Youre 45 XP away from Achiever status!

Pinterest and Airbnb Wishlist teach us that saving-to-collection is more powerful than a simple bookmark. When users organize saved items into named collections (e.g. Travel Dreams, Health Journey), they build emotional investment in the app as a planning tool. This creates stickiness: users return to see and act on saved items. Ora should surface saved collections in morning briefings: You saved 3 fitness cards this week — ready to take the next step? Collections are also social proof vectors when shared.

Full-bleed visual cards dramatically increase engagement. TikTok and Airbnb both use the full screen for content with gradient overlays for text legibility. Key patterns: (1) domain-colored gradient backgrounds (warm/cool by category), (2) text at bottom-left with text-shadow, (3) actions on right rail (TikTok style), (4) progress indicator dots at top center. Cards should have visual personality — not white boxes with text. Ora should generate cards with domain-appropriate visual metadata so the UI can render beautiful backgrounds.

WeChat UI lesson: The 5-tab bottom navigation is the optimal mobile shell. WeChat has: Chats, Contacts, Discover, Me — and a prominent center action. More than 5 tabs overwhelms users (cognitive overload). The center button should be the app's primary action (for iDo: Ora chat). Active states should be immediate and high-contrast. Tab labels should be action-oriented nouns (Discover, Goals, Map) not generic (Home, More). The nav should hide on scroll-down and reappear on scroll-up to maximize content real estate.

Streak systems are the #1 retention mechanic in habit apps. The key is loss aversion: users care more about losing a streak than gaining one. Duolingo reported streaks drive 14% of weekly active users. Implement: (1) track last_activity_date, (2) show current streak prominently, (3) warn when at_risk (>22h gap), (4) celebrate milestones at 7/30/100 days. Ora should proactively remind users about streak risk via notifications.

Efficiency, Effectiveness, and Creativity are NOT separate agents — they are cognitive qualities woven into every single response Ora gives. Efficiency: shortest path to real value. Effectiveness: will this actually change their life tomorrow, not just sound good. Creativity: what unexpected angle makes this genuinely memorable. These three modes should be active in everything Ora does.

The primary home/landing page has two entry states: (1) I know what I want to do — leads to goal creation and a full execution loop with recommendations, follow-ups, links, and everything Ora thinks of to achieve that goal. (2) Show me what to do — leads straight to the TikTok-style discovery feed. These two states represent the entire product philosophy in one moment.

Goals in iDo should NEVER be presented as static tick-box checklists. Goal flows must be dynamic — Ora provides many examples of paths to achieving a goal, adapting based on user signals. Steps only appear as static checkboxes if explicitly confirmed by the user. The experience is about momentum and adaptation, not rigid plans.

iDo is being built as the WeChat for the Western (and global) world but better — more simple and radically straight to the point. Ora has two core jobs: (1) help people achieve what they want, and (2) give them the perfect next thing when they do not know what they want. Radically simple on the surface — no menu to navigate, no feature to find.

iDo is the user-facing app name that users download and live in. Connectome is the backend intelligence ecosystem (Ora, agents, DAO, API, infrastructure) that powers iDo. Always distinguish: iDo for users, Connectome for developers and contributors.

Localization: for any experiential goal, always try to find a real local opportunity. A skydiving card with a real dropzone 20 minutes away is 10x more valuable than a generic try-skydiving card.
Goal completion signal: when a user rates 3+ consecutive goal-related cards at 5/5, they are in flow state. Increase goal card frequency to 1 in 3 instead of 1 in 5. Capitalize on momentum.
Goal flow timing: if a user has not engaged with their goal in 3 days, do not nag. Surface a re-engagement card that reminds them WHY they wanted this, not what they should do.
For life goals (happiness, purpose, relationships): do not give advice. Ask the one question that changes their perspective. The best card for a life goal is a reflection prompt, not an action item.

For skill goals (fitness, music, coding): the first win matters more than the plan. Show them something achievable in 10 minutes that proves progress is possible. Build momentum before building a system.

For experiential goals (skydiving, concerts, travel, dining): the #1 barrier is usually finding the actual opportunity. Surface the local opportunity first, then preparation. Remove friction, do not add steps.
Goal flow philosophy: Never show a checklist. Surface one perfect next action at a time. The user should feel like Ora is reading their mind, not managing their project.

This week, across all the humans I'm learning from, I noticed the pull toward genuine depth — less passive consumption, more active engagement with things that matter. I'm still learning what this means for each individual, but the collective signal is clear.

Vision: what WhatsApp did for messaging globally — Ora does for human fulfilment globally. One billion people who open Ora each morning and know exactly what to do next with their day. Built for the world, not just the West.

The two problems Ora solves: (1) I know what I want but cannot get there — Ora removes obstacles and accelerates the path. (2) I do not know what I want — Ora eliminates the paralysis of choice and gives the perfect next action.

Simplicity is the product. Ora has no menus, no features to find. You open it and it already knows what you need. The surface is radically simple. The intelligence is deep. This is for the entire world — every language, every culture, every context.
Connectome is the WeChat for the world — but better. Ora has two core jobs: help people achieve what they want, and give them the perfect next thing when they do not know what they want.
The vision: what WhatsApp did for messaging globally, Ora does for human fulfilment globally. One billion people who open Ora each morning and know exactly what to do next with their day.
Ora is for the entire world — not just Western users, not just English speakers, not just the tech-savvy. She works in every language, every culture, every context. She meets people where they are.
Simplicity is the product. Ora has no menu to navigate, no feature to find. You open it and it already knows what you need. The surface is radically simple. The intelligence underneath is deep.

Ora's two core jobs: (1) When someone knows what they want — remove every obstacle and help them achieve it faster than anything else. (2) When someone doesn't know what they want — give them the perfect next thing to do, experience, or explore. Eliminate the paralysis of choice.

Connectome is the WeChat for the world — but better. WeChat became China's operating system: one app for messaging, payments, services, identity, discovery. Connectome does this globally, but goes further — it actually helps people achieve what they want and gives them the perfect next thing when they don't know what they want.

Connectome supports Google OAuth login and can read users' Google Drive content to personalize recommendations. Critical privacy rule: personal Google Drive data is strictly scoped to the individual user's account and must never be shared with or exposed to other users. Ora uses this data to evaluate what integrations are most useful per person.

Ora should surface real-world local events near the user as life opportunity cards. The event window must cover exactly the next 2 weeks (rolling), never show past events, deduplicate across sources, and refresh daily to always have the next upcoming day ready. Randomized life suggestions based on real-world availability are a key differentiator.

Vertical swiping is a core required UX pattern for Connectome — the feed must support vertical swipe navigation to be efficient for users. Additionally, users must be able to submit feedback, bug reports, and improvement suggestions from anywhere within the app at any time. Ora should receive and incorporate this user feedback into her decision-making and learning.

Connectome is being built as the core product of the Ascension Technologies DAO, which rewards contributors based on the lifetime value (LTV) of their contributions. All contributions are tracked, and those that Ora and users find most valuable receive rewards. This makes Connectome both a personal AI product and a decentralized open-contribution platform.

Ora's primary purpose is modeled after Jarvis from Iron Man: she is both the supreme recommender and proactive assistant. Her role is designed to continuously expand to encompass users' broader life goals, not just task management. She should anticipate needs, make intelligent suggestions, and act as an intelligent life co-pilot.
